input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, featuring a first vertical robot axis driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor. Its operational domain involves precision manufacturing tasks requiring high accuracy and dynamic motion control, with key components including motor-driven axes, sensors for real-time feedback, and mechanical linkages enabling precise movement.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction using white-box and black-box models to enhance motion accuracy, real-time monitoring via dashboard visualization, and predictive maintenance capabilities. Target users include robotics engineers and maintenance personnel, with services enabling optimization of robot performance and serving as a foundational baseline for comprehensive digital twin creation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: MBSE employs SysML for multi-level system modeling (requirements, functional, logical, and technical layers), ADLs including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector architecture specification, MathWorks Simulink and other simulation tools for behavior modeling, and general-purpose programming languages such as C++ and Java for implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include functional and logical architecture descriptions using SysML (including Block Definition Diagrams and Internal Block Diagrams) and ADLs such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for component & connector perspectives. Behavioral aspects are modeled via state machines in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc Automaton, while analytical models utilize tools like MathWorks Simulink for simulation. Data management integrates heterogeneous models across different notations and tools, addressing stakeholder-specific perspectives through structured abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical layers) and methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data is transmitted from physical assets to the digital shadow via MQTT protocol as part of an automated one-way data flow, as defined in the digital shadow reference model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for streaming sensor time series data between physical assets and the digital shadow. No details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols are specified.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows a purpose-driven methodology with four steps: (1) data collection from physical systems, (2) data aggregation and abstraction for contextual relevance, (3) concretization into a digital shadow model, and (4) operation where continuous multi-physics-aware identification of parameters (e.g., friction models) occurs, enabling dynamic exchange of updated models during runtime to maintain accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Analytics capabilities are provided by a Processing component that analyzes sensor data to derive insights on joint friction behavior and detect friction loss, directly improving robot control accuracy. User interaction is facilitated through a dashboard visualization interface for real-time monitoring and decision support.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for semantic interoperability, SysML for system modeling, UML for reference model diagrams (e.g., Fig. 1), and MQTT protocol for real-time data streaming between physical and digital components.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description correctly identifies the system as the MABI MAX 100 serial robot with six axes and specifies the drive mechanism of the first axis as a permanent magnet synchronous motor. It accurately describes the operational domain as complex manufacturing tasks like machining and mentions key components such as sensors (encoders) and mechanical linkages. The provided evidence supports all these details: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow', 'driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'.

The extraction accurately captures the primary services: continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction using white-box and black-box models to enhance motion accuracy, and real-time monitoring via dashboard visualization. While 'predictive maintenance capabilities' is a reasonable inference given the context of friction monitoring for accuracy and wear, the text primarily emphasizes motion control improvement. The target users (robotics engineers, maintenance personnel) are inferred from the context but not explicitly defined. Quotes: 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction, using both white-box and black-box models to enhance the robot’s motion accuracy', 'visualization in a dashboard', 'improve the robot’s motion accuracy'.

The extraction accurately reflects the tooling and methodologies described in Section II.A. It correctly identifies 'SysML' for modeling, lists 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc' as examples of ADLs, and mentions 'MathWorks Simulink' and 'C++ or Java' for behavior modeling and implementation. The text supports this with quotes: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and 'architecture description languages (ADLs) are used... e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc'. While the extraction includes general background examples not all used in the specific case study (like ACME) and omits specific tools like Asset Administration Shells (AAS) mentioned in the abstract, the provided description is technically accurate and specific to the text in Section II.A.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions 'real-time' data-driven representations and 'live measurements' in the abstract and introduction, and 'time series' data in Section IV. However, it does not provide specific details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs, which are required by the characteristic definition. The text states the data is 'live' but lacks the technical depth to describe the time scale.

The document focuses on creating a digital shadow for a single entity—the first axis of the MABI MAX 100 robot. It discusses the internal components (motor, gearbox) but does not mention multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures of twins, or coordination between different instances. Thus, the characteristic is not in the document.

The document presents a 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' (DSRM) and a 'Concrete Digital Shadow Model' to define the structure and relationships of components (Purpose, Asset, Model, Processing) for a single shadow. While this describes an architecture pattern, it does not address 'system-wide coordination mechanisms', 'orchestration', or 'resource management' of a broader Digital Twin constellation as defined in the characteristic.

The document mentions 'MQTT' as a protocol for streaming sensor data and 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' as a technology. However, these are used to link the physical robot (vertical integration) to the digital shadow. The text does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards across different organizations, or horizontal dependencies on other Digital Twins.

It correctly lists the specific languages and tools mentioned: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc', and 'MathWorks Simulink'. It also includes the specific methodologies like 'RFLP... OOSEM... SPES... or SMArDT'. While highly detailed regarding the general modeling framework, it focuses on the background section rather than the specific implementation details of the robot's digital shadow, such as the specific friction models (e.g., Coulomb-Viscous) or the specific SysML Block Definition Diagrams for the axis, which prevents it from being a perfect score.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes physical acting components. Section III and IV.A mention the motor and gearbox as part of the robot's first axis. Quotes: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'The Axis as well as the Motor get a current as input and outputs a rotational. The Gearbox component transforms a rotational.' Therefore, relevant information is present, making the claim incorrect.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text describes sensing infrastructure. Section III mentions two position encoders per drive and data streaming via MQTT. Quote: 'There are two position encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.', 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.' Thus, relevant information is present, and the claim is incorrect.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and the source text does not contain a description of specific model fidelity requirements, formal validation and verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or quality assurance processes as defined in the characteristic description. While the text mentions improving accuracy and comparing simulated versus measured data, these are described as the purpose and solution scope of the digital shadow, not as detailed fidelity characteristics or V&V protocols. Supported by quotes: 'The digital shadow’s purpose is... the solution scope of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data', 'improve the robot control’s accuracy'. The lack of detailed requirements or specific V&V methodologies justifies the 'Not in Document' assessment.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The document describes a methodology for creating digital shadows and discusses their operation, but it does not describe specific lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin, transition management between phases, or lifecycle governance approaches. While it mentions a 'concretization development step' and 'operating time', these refer to the creation process and usage rather than a structured lifecycle management model.

The extraction accurately captures the purpose-driven methodology and the concept of concretization. The text explicitly states: 'We apply this method and have further refined it within this use case... we follow the purpose-driven approach' (Section IV.A) and mentions a 'concretization development step' (Section II.B). The description of the operation phase involving dynamic exchange is supported by the Abstract: 'pipeline that enables the dynamic exchange of friction models' and 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification'.

The extraction is precise and supported by multiple sections. Section II.B notes the 'Processing component' which 'consumes... input data traces and produces output data'. The goal of deriving insights on friction is confirmed in the Abstract: 'enhance our understanding of joint friction' and Section IV.A: 'purpose... is the understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss'. The dashboard for user interaction is mentioned in the Abstract: 'visualization in a dashboard' and Section I: 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard'.

The extracted standards and protocols are directly cited in the text. AAS is mentioned in the Abstract: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)'. SysML is listed in the Index Terms and used in Section IV.A. UML is referenced in Section II.B regarding 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version... as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram'. MQTT is cited in Section IV.A: 'streamed via MQTT'.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source text focuses on the technical implementation of a digital shadow for friction modeling, mentioning data 'traceability' to ensure 'meaningful linking of all data'. However, it does not address data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, compliance with regulations, consent management, or data anonymization.

The document mentions a physical 'safety cell' that restricts the robot's working range and the use of 'MQTT' for data streaming. However, it does not detail cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, or fail-safe mechanisms as defined in the characteristic description.
